critic_raw: The proposed anomaly detection method raises several significant methodological concerns. First, the Memory Module design assumes a fixed number of memory slots can capture the diversity of normal patterns, but the paper does not provide analysis of sensitivity to this hyperparameter nor a principled method for choosing it. If too few slots are used, normal patterns may be poorly represented; too many may allow anomalous patterns to also be memorized. Second, the hybrid abnormality score combining PSNR and L2 distance between queries and memory items introduces an ad hoc fusion without a justified weighting scheme or ablation study confirming each component's individual contribution. The two signals may be redundant or even conflicting. Third, the Test-Time Updation strategy for updating memory items at inference time risks contaminating the memory with anomalous content if anomalies appear during the test sequence—the paper does not clearly describe a mechanism to prevent this. Fourth, the Separateness and Compactness losses are presented as novel objectives without comparison to standard alternatives such as contrastive loss or clustering-based regularizers, making it unclear whether the added complexity is justified. Fifth, the evaluation relies on PSNR-based metrics which are known to be poorly correlated with human perception and with downstream anomaly detection performance; the absence of alternative evaluation metrics weakens the empirical validation.
all_issues_raised: Memory slot count hyperparameter lacks sensitivity analysis and principled selection method
Hybrid PSNR+L2 abnormality score uses ad hoc fusion without ablation of individual components
Test-Time Updation risks memory contamination by anomalous frames during inference
Separateness and Compactness losses lack comparison to standard alternatives (e.g., contrastive loss)
PSNR-based evaluation metrics are poorly correlated with true anomaly detection performance

Fourth, the Separateness and Compactness losses are presented as novel objectives without comparison to standard alternatives such as contrastive loss or clustering-based regularizers, making it unclear whether the added complexity is justified. Fifth, the evaluation relies on PSNR-based metrics which are known to be poorly correlated with human perception and with downstream anomaly detection performance; the absence of alternative evaluation metrics weakens the empirical validation.
round_transcripts.defender: The memory slot count concern, while valid in principle, is addressed implicitly by the architecture's training dynamics: the Compactness Loss encourages memory items to cluster around representative normal patterns, so the effective number of utilized slots self-regulates. Ablation studies included in the supplementary material show that performance is stable across a range of slot counts, addressing the sensitivity concern adequately. Regarding the hybrid abnormality score: PSNR captures global reconstruction quality while L2-to-memory captures feature-space deviation—these are complementary signals at different abstraction levels, and the authors provide per-component ablations in their experiments. The Test-Time Updation concern is serious but partially addressed: the strategy updates memory only when the reconstruction quality exceeds a threshold, providing an implicit filter against anomalous contamination. The Separateness/Compactness loss comparison to alternatives is a fair criticism—this comparison is not provided. For evaluation, the paper does report frame-level AUC alongside PSNR metrics, which is the standard in the video anomaly detection literature; the use of PSNR is a secondary diagnostic, not the primary performance measure.

round_transcripts.critic: Two issues remain unresolved. First, the defense of the Separateness and Compactness losses concedes the lack of ablation against alternatives—this is a genuine gap. Without showing these losses outperform simpler regularizers, the novelty claim is unsupported. Second, the Test-Time Updation threshold mechanism is described in only qualitative terms: the defense says reconstruction quality must exceed a threshold, but the threshold's derivation, sensitivity, and interaction with anomaly prevalence in the test set are unexplained. A high anomaly-prevalence test sequence would systematically bias memory, degrading the method at exactly the conditions where it matters most.
round_transcripts.defender: On the loss function comparison: the training curves and memory visualization experiments demonstrate qualitatively that Separateness and Compactness produce more diverse and compact memory representations than when either loss is ablated—even if no direct comparison to contrastive loss is made, the ablation within the paper's own framework establishes the marginal value of each component. On the Test-Time Updation threshold: the threshold is set relative to training-time reconstruction distributions, making it adaptive to each scene's normal reconstruction quality. In practice, the benchmark datasets used (CUHK Avenue, UCSD Ped) have low anomaly prevalence, so contamination risk is minimal. The concern about high-prevalence scenarios is theoretically valid but does not apply to the evaluated benchmarks.

all_issues_adjudicated: Separateness and Compactness losses lack comparison to standard alternatives (e.g., contrastive loss)
Test-Time Updation threshold derivation and behavior under high anomaly prevalence remain underspecified

critic_raw: The GAN2Shape methodology presents several methodological concerns. First, the method depends entirely on a pre-trained StyleGAN2 model and its learned latent space structure—the quality of 3D shape recovery is therefore bounded by the diversity and coverage of the training data for that GAN. The paper does not analyze how distribution shift between GAN training data and target images affects shape recovery accuracy. Second, the claim that varying the w-vector generates valid multi-view and multi-lighting samples is assumed but not theoretically justified: StyleGAN2's latent space does not factorize lighting from geometry, and entanglement between these factors could produce incorrect 3D interpretations. Third, the evaluation metrics for 3D shape recovery are not clearly specified—using visual inspection or downstream task proxies instead of standard 3D shape metrics (Chamfer distance, surface normal error) makes quantitative comparison with other methods difficult. Fourth, the claim that the method generalizes across object categories (faces, cats, cars, buildings) is supported by separate per-category experiments but there is no unified evaluation or analysis of failure modes across categories. Fifth, the claim of unsupervised 3D reconstruction 'without restrictive domain assumptions' is potentially misleading: the method inherits all inductive biases from StyleGAN2's training, including its dataset-specific priors.
all_issues_raised: Method performance is bounded by StyleGAN2 training data and distribution shift to target images is unanalyzed
Latent space variation does not factorize lighting from geometry, introducing entanglement artifacts
Evaluation lacks standard 3D metrics (Chamfer distance, surface normal error)
Generalization claim across categories lacks unified evaluation and failure mode analysis
Unsupervised claim is misleading given inherited StyleGAN2 dataset-specific priors

First, the method depends entirely on a pre-trained StyleGAN2 model and its learned latent space structure—the quality of 3D shape recovery is therefore bounded by the diversity and coverage of the training data for that GAN. The paper does not analyze how distribution shift between GAN training data and target images affects shape recovery accuracy. Second, the claim that varying the w-vector generates valid multi-view and multi-lighting samples is assumed but not theoretically justified: StyleGAN2's latent space does not factorize lighting from geometry, and entanglement between these factors could produce incorrect 3D interpretations. Third, the evaluation metrics for 3D shape recovery are not clearly specified—using visual inspection or downstream task proxies instead of standard 3D shape metrics (Chamfer distance, surface normal error) makes quantitative comparison with other methods difficult. Fourth, the claim that the method generalizes across object categories (faces, cats, cars, buildings) is supported by separate per-category experiments but there is no unified evaluation or analysis of failure modes across categories. Fifth, the claim of unsupervised 3D reconstruction 'without restrictive domain assumptions' is potentially misleading: the method inherits all inductive biases from StyleGAN2's training, including its dataset-specific priors.
round_transcripts.defender: The dependency on StyleGAN2 is a design choice, not a flaw: GAN2Shape explicitly positions itself as a method that exploits an existing generative model's learned knowledge, analogous to transfer learning. The distribution shift question is addressed by showing that the method works on real images mapped into the GAN's latent space via GAN inversion. On lighting-geometry entanglement: while StyleGAN2 does not explicitly factorize these, the paper uses a photometric rendering model to impose physical consistency during shape estimation, providing an explicit disentanglement mechanism. The per-category evaluations are the appropriate granularity since shape recovery quality differs by object type; aggregate metrics would be misleading. The evaluation metric concern is valid—quantitative 3D metrics are limited in the paper. The unsupervised characterization is standard in the literature and refers to absence of 3D supervision, not absence of 2D training data.

round_transcripts.critic: Three issues remain insufficiently resolved. First, the photometric rendering model as disentanglement mechanism is asserted but its completeness is not proven—entanglement in the latent space could still introduce systematic shape errors that the rendering constraint does not fully correct, and the paper lacks ablation showing the rendering constraint actually eliminates entanglement artifacts. Second, the quantitative 3D metrics gap remains: the defense acknowledges this limitation without resolving it, meaning the paper's empirical claims about 3D accuracy are not independently verifiable. Third, while the reframing of 'unsupervised' as 'no 3D supervision' is standard usage, the practical implication that StyleGAN2's learned priors constrain generalization to the types of objects it was trained on is a meaningful limitation not discussed in the paper.
round_transcripts.defender: On disentanglement: the paper provides visual comparisons of re-lit and rotated objects showing geometrically consistent results; the rendering model's photometric constraints are the standard approach in shape-from-shading literature and their effectiveness is empirically demonstrated. On 3D metrics: the paper compares against BFM (Basel Face Model) for faces using landmark-based alignment, providing a quantitative anchor; for other categories, visual quality metrics are the field norm due to the absence of ground-truth 3D for natural images. The StyleGAN2-inherited prior is a known property of the method and is discussed as a design choice with qualitative results demonstrating generalization within supported categories.

all_issues_adjudicated: Entanglement between lighting and geometry in latent space is not rigorously ablated away
Quantitative 3D evaluation (Chamfer distance, surface normal error) is absent or limited to faces only

critic_raw: The privacy-preserving transformation policy search methodology has several notable weaknesses. First, the two metrics S_pri and S_acc are introduced as proxies for reconstruction attack success and model accuracy, but their validity as proxies is not rigorously established—specifically, S_pri measures privacy against a specific type of reconstruction attack, and the claim that policies found using this metric 'defeat various types of reconstruction attacks' requires demonstration against diverse attack families not used during policy search. Second, the search algorithm operates over discrete transformation policies, but the space of possible policies is combinatorially large; the paper does not analyze search completeness or the risk of finding suboptimal policies that happen to score well on S_pri but fail against unseen attacks. Third, the claim of transferability between datasets is tested on a limited number of dataset pairs, and the mechanism of transferability is not explained—it is unclear whether certain data characteristics make transfer more or less reliable. Fourth, the assertion that privacy policies have 'negligible impact on training efficiency' needs quantification; 'negligible' is subjective without a formal threshold or statistical test. Fifth, the paper does not analyze the adversarial robustness of the found policies themselves—whether an adaptive attacker who knows the transformation policy can still reconstruct training data.
all_issues_raised: S_pri metric validity as proxy for diverse attack types not rigorously established
Policy search space is combinatorially large with no completeness or optimality guarantees
Transferability claim is tested on limited dataset pairs without mechanistic explanation
Negligible accuracy impact claim lacks formal quantification or statistical threshold
No analysis of adaptive attackers who know the transformation policy

Fifth, the paper does not analyze the adversarial robustness of the found policies themselves—whether an adaptive attacker who knows the transformation policy can still reconstruct training data.
round_transcripts.defender: The evaluation against multiple attack types directly addresses the generalization concern: the paper tests policies found with one attack type against gradient inversion and deep leakage attacks, demonstrating cross-attack robustness empirically. The search space concern is acknowledged in the paper, which uses a greedy search heuristic that the authors validate produces policies competitive with exhaustive search on small policy spaces; the heuristic is the standard approach in NAS-inspired policy search. On transferability: the paper demonstrates across CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet that policies transfer well, with the mechanism being that effective augmentations obfuscate semantic features rather than dataset-specific features. On negligible accuracy impact: the paper provides accuracy delta tables with statistical significance tests. The adaptive attacker concern is a valid open question that the authors acknowledge as future work.

round_transcripts.critic: Two issues remain. First, the cross-attack evaluation provides empirical evidence but does not resolve the theoretical gap: S_pri is optimized for one attack family, and while the results are encouraging, the claim of defeating 'various types' of attacks is broader than the tested set. The defense relies on empirical results across a small number of attack variants, which is insufficient to make a general claim. Second, the adaptive attacker concern was conceded as future work—this is a significant limitation because real-world adversaries in federated learning are not assumed to be oblivious to the defense mechanism. The omission of this analysis weakens the practical privacy guarantee substantially.
round_transcripts.defender: On the attack generalization point: the attacks tested (DLG, iDLG, CPL) represent the dominant families of gradient reconstruction attacks; testing across this set is sufficient for the paper's scope, which is a workshop/conference contribution rather than a comprehensive survey. The adaptive attacker analysis is a legitimate future work item that does not invalidate the demonstrated effectiveness against the tested attack classes; the paper never claims formal cryptographic privacy guarantees, only practical effectiveness against known attacks. Framing the contribution within its stated scope, both remaining critiques are partially addressed by the empirical evidence presented.

all_issues_adjudicated: Generalization claim to 'various attack types' exceeds what the tested attack set supports
Adaptive attacker robustness is unanalyzed, leaving the practical privacy guarantee unquantified

critic_raw: The unrolled network phase retrieval approach has several methodological issues. First, the claim that the learned reference generalizes to unseen data distributions is evaluated on a limited set of test conditions; the paper does not specify the statistical distance between training and test distributions, making the generalization claim hard to evaluate rigorously. Second, the iterative unrolling approach inherits the computational cost of the underlying phase retrieval iterations while adding learned parameters—the paper does not provide a systematic comparison of computation time versus reconstruction quality tradeoffs against pure algorithmic baselines. Third, the robustness claim regarding spatial augmentations (shifting and rotation) is tested on a narrow set of augmentation types and magnitudes; extreme shifts or rotations may degrade performance, and the boundary conditions are not analyzed. Fourth, the use of a single reference image introduces a single-point-of-failure: the quality of reconstruction depends heavily on how well the reference captures the signal's statistics, yet the paper provides limited analysis of reference quality sensitivity. Fifth, the evaluation is performed on open-access datasets without cross-dataset validation, raising questions about whether reported performance metrics reflect genuine generalization or dataset-specific fitting.
all_issues_raised: Generalization to unseen distributions lacks statistical characterization of distribution shift
Computation-quality tradeoff versus algorithmic baselines not systematically compared
Robustness to spatial augmentations tested on narrow range of magnitudes without boundary analysis
Sensitivity to reference image quality is underanalyzed
Single-dataset evaluation limits evidence of true generalization

round_transcripts.critic: The unrolled network phase retrieval approach has several methodological issues. First, the claim that the learned reference generalizes to unseen data distributions is evaluated on a limited set of test conditions; the paper does not specify the statistical distance between training and test distributions, making the generalization claim hard to evaluate rigorously. Third, the robustness claim regarding spatial augmentations (shifting and rotation) is tested on a narrow set of augmentation types and magnitudes; extreme shifts or rotations may degrade performance, and the boundary conditions are not analyzed. Fourth, the use of a single reference image introduces a single-point-of-failure: the quality of reconstruction depends heavily on how well the reference captures the signal's statistics, yet the paper provides limited analysis of reference quality sensitivity. Fifth, the evaluation is performed on open-access datasets without cross-dataset validation, raising questions about whether reported performance metrics reflect genuine generalization or dataset-specific fitting.
round_transcripts.defender: The generalization claim is supported by training on one dataset and testing on a held-out set drawn from a different distribution—the paper explicitly tests on out-of-distribution natural images not seen during training. The computational comparison is addressed through FLOP counts and runtime tables comparing the unrolled method to FISTA and other iterative baselines; the key finding is that fewer iterations are needed at inference time because the learned reference warm-starts the reconstruction. The augmentation robustness tests cover translation shifts up to 25% of image width and rotations up to 45 degrees, which are substantial ranges covering practical deployment conditions. Reference quality sensitivity is tested through experiments with random, learned, and oracle references showing that the learned reference substantially outperforms random while approaching oracle performance. The single-dataset concern is partially valid—the paper uses CelebA and MNIST as evaluation sets, providing two distinct domains.

round_transcripts.critic: Two issues remain unresolved. First, while two datasets are used, CelebA and MNIST represent very different domains but both are well-studied benchmarks; the statistical characterization of train/test distribution shift is still absent. Claiming generalization without measuring the gap between distributions is a qualitative assertion. Second, the reference quality sensitivity analysis compares random versus learned versus oracle, but does not analyze degraded or adversarially chosen references—understanding the failure modes of reference selection is important for practical deployment where reference selection may not be controlled.
round_transcripts.defender: The two-dataset evaluation spanning continuous natural images (CelebA) and discrete digit images (MNIST) provides meaningful evidence of generalization across structurally different image statistics—quantifying the distributional gap precisely is not standard practice in the phase retrieval literature and is not required to validate the core contribution. The reference sensitivity analysis is complete within the paper's scope: random reference establishes a lower bound, oracle establishes an upper bound, and the learned reference is shown to be near-oracle. Adversarially constructed references are an edge case beyond the paper's stated problem setting. Both remaining critiques represent scope extensions rather than methodological flaws in the reported experiments.

all_issues_adjudicated: Generalization claim lacks statistical characterization of train-to-test distribution shift
Reference quality failure modes and adversarial reference sensitivity unanalyzed

critic_raw: The Hamiltonian Generative Networks methodology raises multiple important concerns. First, the claim of learning Hamiltonian dynamics 'without restrictive domain assumptions' is overstated: the architecture encodes strong inductive biases toward energy-conserving systems. Real physical systems have dissipation, friction, and non-conservative forces that violate Hamiltonian assumptions; the method's applicability outside ideal conservative systems is not examined. Second, the evaluation is conducted only on simple toy physical systems (pendulum, spring-mass, n-body), all of which have well-known analytic solutions; no evaluation on more complex or partially-observable physical systems is provided, limiting the practical significance of results. Third, the generative rollout quality is measured visually and via pixel-level reconstruction metrics, but the paper does not test whether the learned dynamics obey physical conservation laws quantitatively (e.g., energy conservation error over long rollouts). Fourth, the variational training objective introduces a posterior collapse risk, especially for short observation sequences; the paper does not analyze whether the latent dynamical representation is meaningfully used or whether the model degenerates to a pixel-level predictor. Fifth, forward and backward rollout symmetry is claimed but not quantitatively evaluated—time-reversibility is a key property of Hamiltonian systems and should be evaluated explicitly.
all_issues_raised: Hamiltonian inductive bias precludes dissipative or non-conservative physical systems, limiting claimed generality
Evaluation only on simple toy systems with known analytic solutions
Physical conservation law adherence (e.g., energy conservation) not quantitatively evaluated over long rollouts
Posterior collapse risk in variational objective not analyzed
Time-reversibility (forward/backward symmetry) not quantitatively evaluated

round_transcripts.critic: The Hamiltonian Generative Networks methodology raises multiple important concerns. First, the claim of learning Hamiltonian dynamics 'without restrictive domain assumptions' is overstated: the architecture encodes strong inductive biases toward energy-conserving systems. Real physical systems have dissipation, friction, and non-conservative forces that violate Hamiltonian assumptions; the method's applicability outside ideal conservative systems is not examined. Second, the evaluation is conducted only on simple toy physical systems (pendulum, spring-mass, n-body), all of which have well-known analytic solutions; no evaluation on more complex or partially-observable physical systems is provided, limiting the practical significance of results. Third, the generative rollout quality is measured visually and via pixel-level reconstruction metrics, but the paper does not test whether the learned dynamics obey physical conservation laws quantitatively (e.g., energy conservation error over long rollouts). Fourth, the variational training objective introduces a posterior collapse risk, especially for short observation sequences; the paper does not analyze whether the latent dynamical representation is meaningfully used or whether the model degenerates to a pixel-level predictor. Fifth, forward and backward rollout symmetry is claimed but not quantitatively evaluated—time-reversibility is a key property of Hamiltonian systems and should be evaluated explicitly.
round_transcripts.defender: The 'no restrictive domain assumptions' claim refers to the absence of assumptions about the specific form of the Hamiltonian (not that the method applies outside Hamiltonian mechanics)—this is a standard clarification in physics-informed ML. The toy system evaluation is deliberate: systems with known analytic solutions allow controlled comparison of learned dynamics against ground truth, which is the appropriate validation strategy for a new method. Energy conservation is evaluated implicitly through long-rollout trajectory comparisons with analytic solutions; the latent representation is analyzed through interpolation experiments confirming smooth and meaningful latent dynamics. Posterior collapse is monitored through the ELBO terms and the latent trajectory visualizations show clear dynamical structure. Time-reversibility is demonstrated qualitatively through backward rollout examples.

round_transcripts.critic: Three issues persist. First, the scope clarification on 'no domain assumptions' is reasonable but the defense still does not address whether the method can handle even mildly non-conservative systems (damped pendulum), which are more realistic than the pure Hamiltonian toy cases. Second, energy conservation is evaluated only implicitly through trajectory comparison—there is no explicit measurement of energy drift over time steps, which would quantify the model's physical fidelity. Third, time-reversibility is shown only qualitatively; a quantitative metric (e.g., MSE between forward rollout and time-reversed backward rollout) is absent.
round_transcripts.defender: On non-conservative systems: the method is explicitly designed for Hamiltonian systems; applying it to dissipative systems would be a misuse of the framework, not a methodological flaw. The paper is transparent about this scope. On energy conservation metrics: trajectory MSE against analytic solutions implicitly captures energy drift because energy errors accumulate as trajectory deviation; the absence of a separate energy-drift plot is a presentation choice, not a measurement gap. On time-reversibility quantification: the qualitative backward rollout demonstrations are compelling and consistent with the paper's scope as a generative model paper rather than a physics simulation benchmark paper.

all_issues_adjudicated: Energy conservation adherence not explicitly quantified (energy drift over rollout steps)
Time-reversibility not quantitatively evaluated

critic_raw: The Graph Edit Network methodology has several methodological concerns. First, the evaluation uses only synthetic datasets generated specifically for the paper's experiments; the absence of real-world graph datasets limits the assessment of practical performance and introduces the risk that the synthetic data is implicitly tailored to the method's strengths. Second, the claim of scalability to larger graphs is asserted without quantitative benchmarks: computational complexity analysis is needed to substantiate this claim, including both theoretical complexity (e.g., O(|V|^2) or better) and empirical runtime on increasingly large graphs. Third, the GEN architecture is presented as a general output layer, but the task experiments are limited in diversity—all tasks involve deterministic graph transformations; stochastic or partial-information tasks are not explored. Fourth, the comparison baseline set is weak: the paper compares against simple non-learned graph editing approaches but not against other learned graph transformation methods or message-passing networks with similar output layers. Fifth, the training methodology on synthetic data raises the question of how the model handles combinatorial ambiguity in graph editing—multiple valid edit sequences may produce the same target graph, and the paper does not address how this non-uniqueness affects training stability.
all_issues_raised: Evaluation exclusively on synthetic datasets, no real-world graph benchmarks
Scalability claim lacks quantitative complexity analysis and empirical runtime benchmarks
Task diversity is limited to deterministic graph transformations only
Baseline comparison set is weak, lacking comparison to learned graph transformation methods
Training on non-unique edit sequences may create instability; this is not addressed

round_transcripts.critic: The Graph Edit Network methodology has several methodological concerns. First, the evaluation uses only synthetic datasets generated specifically for the paper's experiments; the absence of real-world graph datasets limits the assessment of practical performance and introduces the risk that the synthetic data is implicitly tailored to the method's strengths. Second, the claim of scalability to larger graphs is asserted without quantitative benchmarks: computational complexity analysis is needed to substantiate this claim, including both theoretical complexity (e.g., O(|V|^2) or better) and empirical runtime on increasingly large graphs. Third, the GEN architecture is presented as a general output layer, but the task experiments are limited in diversity—all tasks involve deterministic graph transformations; stochastic or partial-information tasks are not explored. Fourth, the comparison baseline set is weak: the paper compares against simple non-learned graph editing approaches but not against other learned graph transformation methods or message-passing networks with similar output layers. Fifth, the training methodology on synthetic data raises the question of how the model handles combinatorial ambiguity in graph editing—multiple valid edit sequences may produce the same target graph, and the paper does not address how this non-uniqueness affects training stability.
round_transcripts.defender: Synthetic datasets are the appropriate evaluation choice for a method that introduces a novel output layer: synthetic data provides controlled task properties that allow precise measurement of the model's ability to learn the target transformation rule, which is impossible with noisy real-world graphs. The scalability claim is supported by experiments showing training and inference on graphs with up to several hundred nodes and edges with stable performance; the paper provides runtime tables across graph sizes. The deterministic transformation focus is intentional for the initial presentation—the authors establish the concept with well-defined tasks before extension to stochastic settings. Baseline selection is appropriate for a proof-of-concept paper introducing a new architectural component rather than a systems benchmark paper. The combinatorial ambiguity issue is handled by training with a specific edit sequence target derived from a minimum edit distance oracle, ensuring training consistency.

round_transcripts.critic: Two issues remain. First, the synthetic-only evaluation defense is reasonable for a proof of concept, but the paper makes claims about GEN as a practical output layer for graph neural networks—this broader claim requires at least one real-world task demonstration. The defense justifies the design choice but does not resolve the gap between claimed applicability and demonstrated applicability. Second, while the minimum edit distance oracle provides training consistency, the paper does not analyze sensitivity to oracle quality or alternative oracle definitions—different edit distance metrics may produce different oracles, and the model's performance may be oracle-dependent.
round_transcripts.defender: The practical applicability claim is demonstrated through the diversity of synthetic tasks rather than specific real-world datasets; the method's correctness on well-understood synthetic problems is sufficient for an architectural contribution. Oracle sensitivity is a theoretical concern but in practice minimum edit distance is a well-defined and standard oracle for graph editing tasks; alternative definitions would be non-standard and the results on synthetic tasks with known structure demonstrate robustness to task variation. Both remaining concerns are reasonable research extension suggestions rather than fundamental methodological flaws in the reported work.

all_issues_adjudicated: No real-world graph dataset evaluation despite claims of broad practical applicability
Oracle sensitivity for training target generation is unanalyzed

critic_raw: The Generative Causal Explanations framework has several methodological concerns. First, the distinction between causal and non-causal factors is operationalized through a latent variable model, but the identifiability of this decomposition is not proven—without identifiability guarantees, the 'causal' factors may be arbitrary latent directions that correlate with classifier decisions without causal interpretation. Second, the evaluation on MNIST and fMNIST represents a very restricted domain: both are simple, low-resolution, grayscale image benchmarks where causal factors are easy to define and identify; there is no evaluation on more complex datasets where causal structure is ambiguous. Third, the method requires knowledge of Algorithm 1 to replicate but the description of this algorithm in the paper appears to be insufficiently detailed for independent implementation—reliance on specific GPU hardware (Nvidia GeForce GTX 1080) further constrains reproducibility. Fourth, the claim that the framework provides 'causal' explanations is philosophically imprecise: the method identifies correlational factors that drive classifier decisions, not necessarily counterfactually robust causal factors in the Pearl-Judea sense. Fifth, no user study or human evaluation is conducted to assess whether the explanations are actually interpretable or useful to practitioners.
all_issues_raised: Identifiability of causal vs. non-causal factor decomposition not proven
Evaluation limited to simple MNIST/fMNIST benchmarks, no complex dataset evaluation
Algorithm 1 description may be insufficiently detailed for independent replication
Causal explanation claim is philosophically imprecise (correlational vs. interventional causality)
No human evaluation of explanation interpretability or utility

round_transcripts.critic: The Generative Causal Explanations framework has several methodological concerns. First, the distinction between causal and non-causal factors is operationalized through a latent variable model, but the identifiability of this decomposition is not proven—without identifiability guarantees, the 'causal' factors may be arbitrary latent directions that correlate with classifier decisions without causal interpretation. Second, the evaluation on MNIST and fMNIST represents a very restricted domain: both are simple, low-resolution, grayscale image benchmarks where causal factors are easy to define and identify; there is no evaluation on more complex datasets where causal structure is ambiguous. Third, the method requires knowledge of Algorithm 1 to replicate but the description of this algorithm in the paper appears to be insufficiently detailed for independent implementation—reliance on specific GPU hardware (Nvidia GeForce GTX 1080) further constrains reproducibility. Fourth, the claim that the framework provides 'causal' explanations is philosophically imprecise: the method identifies correlational factors that drive classifier decisions, not necessarily counterfactually robust causal factors in the Pearl-Judea sense. Fifth, no user study or human evaluation is conducted to assess whether the explanations are actually interpretable or useful to practitioners.
round_transcripts.defender: The identifiability concern is addressed within the paper's theoretical framework: the causal decomposition is defined with respect to the specific causal graph structure assumed for the data, and identifiability holds under standard assumptions including sufficient observational support and faithfulness. The MNIST/fMNIST evaluation is appropriate for a method paper introducing a new theoretical framework—the benchmarks allow controlled validation of the core concept. Algorithm 1 is provided with pseudocode and all hyperparameters; GPU specification is provided for reproducibility, not as a constraint. The philosophical precision of 'causal' is addressed in the paper's formal notation which defines causality in terms of the assumed causal graph. Human evaluation is an extension beyond the scope of the technical paper.

round_transcripts.critic: Two issues persist. First, the identifiability defense asserts that it holds under faithfulness and sufficient support assumptions—but these assumptions are not verified or tested in the experiments. In practice, violation of faithfulness is common, and the consequences for the quality of causal explanations are not analyzed. Second, the philosophical precision defense—that causality is defined relative to the assumed causal graph—actually concedes the concern: if the causal graph is assumed rather than learned or verified, then the 'causal' explanations are only as reliable as the assumed graph, and graph misspecification is not analyzed.
round_transcripts.defender: Faithfulness testing is a meta-level statistical question beyond the scope of an explanation method paper; the assumption is standard in causal inference and is not unique to this paper. Causal graph misspecification is a known limitation of all SCM-based methods, and the paper operates within the standard practice of assuming a correct causal graph structure for controlled evaluation. The remaining critiques apply equally to all causal inference methods and do not single out a flaw specific to this paper's contribution.

all_issues_adjudicated: Faithfulness and identifiability assumptions are not verified or tested empirically
Causal graph misspecification risk is unanalyzed

critic_raw: The YNet trajectory forecasting methodology raises several concerns. First, the two-stage prediction approach (goal then waypoints then full trajectory) introduces cascading errors: errors in goal prediction propagate to waypoint selection and then to trajectory completion. The paper does not analyze how this error propagation affects performance relative to single-stage approaches. Second, the use of segmentation maps as input assumes access to semantic scene information at test time—this is a strong assumption not present in all deployment scenarios, and the contribution of segmentation maps versus past trajectory heatmaps alone is not fully ablated. Third, the multi-modal prediction is evaluated using minimum-ADE (minADE) and minimum-FDE (minFDE) metrics which select the best prediction from K samples; this rewards methods that produce diverse but mostly wrong predictions. The paper may not report Top-1 or mean metrics that better reflect single-prediction performance. Fourth, the U-Net-based architecture processes segmentation maps at fixed resolution; pedestrian trajectory prediction in long-range scenarios (large scene scales) may be affected by resolution limitations not analyzed. Fifth, the SDD and ETH/UCY datasets have different coordinate systems and observation conventions; cross-dataset generalization analysis is limited.
all_issues_raised: Cascading error propagation from goal to waypoint to trajectory not analyzed
Segmentation map requirement is a strong assumption; ablation versus trajectory-only input is incomplete
minADE/minFDE metrics favor diversity over accuracy; top-1 metrics not reported
U-Net fixed resolution may limit performance on large-scale scenes
Cross-dataset generalization analysis between SDD and ETH/UCY is limited

round_transcripts.critic: The YNet trajectory forecasting methodology raises several concerns. First, the two-stage prediction approach (goal then waypoints then full trajectory) introduces cascading errors: errors in goal prediction propagate to waypoint selection and then to trajectory completion. The paper does not analyze how this error propagation affects performance relative to single-stage approaches. Second, the use of segmentation maps as input assumes access to semantic scene information at test time—this is a strong assumption not present in all deployment scenarios, and the contribution of segmentation maps versus past trajectory heatmaps alone is not fully ablated. Third, the multi-modal prediction is evaluated using minimum-ADE (minADE) and minimum-FDE (minFDE) metrics which select the best prediction from K samples; this rewards methods that produce diverse but mostly wrong predictions. The paper may not report Top-1 or mean metrics that better reflect single-prediction performance. Fourth, the U-Net-based architecture processes segmentation maps at fixed resolution; pedestrian trajectory prediction in long-range scenarios (large scene scales) may be affected by resolution limitations not analyzed. Fifth, the SDD and ETH/UCY datasets have different coordinate systems and observation conventions; cross-dataset generalization analysis is limited.
round_transcripts.defender: The hierarchical prediction structure is the core contribution: conditioning waypoints on goals reduces the long-range uncertainty of full trajectory prediction, and the ablation of goal-conditioning versus waypoint-only conditioning demonstrates the value of each stage. The segmentation map ablation is included in the paper, showing both with and without map conditions; the paper demonstrates that the segmentation map adds meaningful performance gains that justify the requirement. MinADE/FDE at K=20 is the standard metric in the trajectory prediction literature for multi-modal methods; reporting mean ADE would conflate prediction quality with mode coverage, which is inappropriate for multi-modal approaches. The resolution concern is addressed by the dataset-specific preprocessing that normalizes scene scales. ETH/UCY and SDD are both evaluated with their standard protocols, and the cross-dataset performance is consistent with prior work.

round_transcripts.critic: Two issues remain. First, the cascading error analysis defense argues the hierarchical structure reduces uncertainty without providing a comparison of actual error rates at each stage—the ablation of goal versus waypoint conditioning is not the same as measuring how goal prediction errors propagate into trajectory errors. A concrete error propagation analysis (e.g., oracle goals versus predicted goals) would directly address this. Second, minADE@K=20 being standard does not resolve the concern—it is specifically the combination of standard usage with the claim of outperforming 'existing approaches' that is misleading if other approaches are compared at different K values or different metrics.
round_transcripts.defender: Oracle goal experiments are provided in the supplementary: using ground-truth goals substantially improves trajectory completion performance, confirming the hierarchical design's value and quantifying the gap introduced by goal prediction errors. The metric comparison concern is addressed by the paper's careful comparison table that uses the same K across all compared methods; the outperformance claim is based on consistent metric settings. Both defenses are supported by specific experimental evidence in the paper.

all_issues_adjudicated: Metric consistency across compared methods at same K not independently verifiable without accessing original papers

critic_raw: The strategic classification framework has several methodological concerns. First, the model requires differentiating through strategic user responses—this requires the agent cost model to be differentiable and known during training, but in real deployments, agent cost functions are heterogeneous, unknown, and may be adversarially manipulated. The paper does not analyze performance under cost model misspecification. Second, the regularization strategies for promoting favorable social outcomes require defining what 'favorable' means, which is a normative choice. The paper does not engage with the welfare-utility tradeoffs introduced by regularization or analyze how different regularization strengths affect different population subgroups. Third, the claim that the method 'performs well across a variety of real-life scenarios involving strategic and non-strategic users' is tested on limited benchmark datasets that may not capture real strategic behavior. Fourth, the end-to-end training approach assumes strategic responses at training time are representative of strategic responses at deployment—if the deployment population has different strategic incentives, the model may be miscalibrated. Fifth, the comparison to non-strategic approaches uses a single evaluation metric without considering the distributional impact of strategic classification on agent behavior over time.
all_issues_raised: Cost model differentiability assumption and misspecification risk not analyzed
Normative definition of 'favorable social outcomes' and subgroup welfare impacts not analyzed
Real-life strategic behavior validation on limited benchmarks
Training-deployment distribution shift in strategic incentives not addressed
Comparison to non-strategic approaches uses limited metrics without distributional impact analysis

round_transcripts.defender: The cost model differentiability is a known requirement stated explicitly in the paper; sensitivity to cost model misspecification is analyzed through robustness experiments where the training cost model differs from the test cost model. The social outcome regularization is implemented through explicit fairness constraints, not an implicit normative choice—the paper discusses multiple regularization objectives and allows practitioners to choose based on their fairness definition. The benchmark datasets include semi-synthetic scenarios calibrated to real strategic behavior studies in credit scoring and hiring domains. Training-deployment shift is addressed through the regularization which encourages learned rules that are robust to response heterogeneity. Distributional impact is analyzed through group-level performance metrics across strategic and non-strategic subpopulations.

round_transcripts.critic: Two issues remain insufficiently resolved. First, the cost model misspecification experiments are limited in scope—they test perturbations to a fixed parametric form of the cost function but do not test against fundamentally different cost function families, which would be the realistic misspecification scenario. Second, the benchmark calibration to credit and hiring domains uses semi-synthetic data, which means the strategic behavior is simulated rather than observed; the gap between simulated and real strategic behavior is not quantified.
round_transcripts.defender: The parametric cost misspecification tests are the standard approach in this literature and cover the primary misspecification modes. Testing against fundamentally different cost families would require domain-specific models outside the paper's scope. The semi-synthetic validation is the state of the art for strategic classification research since observing real strategic manipulation in controlled experiments is infeasible; the calibration to real-world scenarios is as close as methodologically possible. Both residual concerns reflect limitations of the research field rather than specific flaws in this paper's methodology.

all_issues_adjudicated: Cost model misspecification to different cost function families unanalyzed
Semi-synthetic to real strategic behavior gap not quantified

critic_raw: The ATSPrivacy framework raises several concerns. First, the privacy score metric S_pri is defined relative to reconstruction quality against a specific attack model; it is unclear whether policies optimized for this metric are robust to different reconstruction attack architectures or gradient inversion techniques developed after the policy search. Second, the augmentation policy space is discrete and finite, but the paper does not analyze whether the found policies represent global optima or are sensitive to search initialization, which is particularly important given the combinatorial nature of the search. Third, the accuracy-privacy tradeoff curve is not comprehensively analyzed: the paper demonstrates that effective privacy policies exist that maintain model accuracy, but does not characterize the Pareto frontier of all policies, making it difficult to assess how tight the tradeoff actually is. Fourth, the evaluation datasets (CIFAR-10/100 and ImageNet subsets) may not represent all data modalities (e.g., medical images, tabular data gradients) where federated learning is most privacy-critical. Fifth, the framework assumes that gradients are the sole communication channel—in practical federated learning with secure aggregation or other privacy mechanisms, the additive benefit of augmentation policies may be diminished.
all_issues_raised: S_pri optimized against a fixed attack model; robustness to newer attack architectures unverified
Policy search lacks global optimality guarantees; initialization sensitivity unanalyzed
Pareto frontier of accuracy-privacy tradeoff not characterized
Evaluation does not cover high-stakes data modalities (medical imaging, tabular data)
Interaction with other federated learning privacy mechanisms not analyzed

round_transcripts.critic: The ATSPrivacy framework raises several concerns. Fifth, the framework assumes that gradients are the sole communication channel—in practical federated learning with secure aggregation or other privacy mechanisms, the additive benefit of augmentation policies may be diminished.
round_transcripts.defender: The attack generalization is tested empirically: the paper evaluates policies found against one gradient reconstruction attack against multiple other attacks (including iDLG and CPL), demonstrating that policies that obscure semantic content generalize across attack families. The discrete policy search is exhaustive within a bounded policy space; the authors verify that their heuristic search finds policies matching exhaustive search on smaller spaces. The Pareto analysis is conducted through multiple policy configurations with varying privacy-accuracy tradeoffs plotted in the paper. The domain limitation is acknowledged; the method's generalizability to non-image data is noted as future work. Interaction with secure aggregation is discussed in the limitations section.

round_transcripts.critic: Two issues remain. First, the Pareto frontier claim requires clarification: plotting several tradeoff points does not characterize the full frontier. Without a continuous or comprehensive analysis of policy configurations, the tightness of the tradeoff is still unclear. Second, the interaction with other privacy mechanisms is mentioned in limitations but not analyzed—in practice, most production federated learning deployments use differential privacy or secure aggregation, and the paper's demonstrated privacy benefits may be redundant or diminished in such settings, which is the practically relevant scenario.
round_transcripts.defender: The Pareto analysis presents the achievable tradeoffs under the proposed framework's policy vocabulary; characterizing a full Pareto frontier would require continuous relaxation of the discrete policy space, which is outside the paper's scope. The interaction with DP and secure aggregation is a deployment consideration addressed in the discussion; the paper's contribution is the augmentation-based policy search mechanism, not a comprehensive comparison to all privacy approaches. The limitations section explicitly discusses these constraints, maintaining scientific honesty about the scope.

all_issues_adjudicated: Pareto frontier of accuracy-privacy tradeoff not fully characterized
Interaction with differential privacy and secure aggregation unanalyzed

critic_raw: The projection-based truncated SVD update algorithm has several methodological issues. First, the paper claims improved computational efficiency but does not provide a rigorous theoretical analysis of the computational complexity as a function of matrix size, rank, and update frequency—without this analysis, the claimed speedups may not generalize beyond the experimental configurations tested. Second, the accuracy of the updated SVD is measured by the reconstruction error between the updated approximation and a fresh full SVD, but the paper does not analyze how approximation errors accumulate over multiple sequential updates, which is the practical use case in streaming data scenarios. Third, the baseline methods selected for comparison may not include the most competitive existing incremental SVD algorithms, raising questions about whether the claimed performance improvements represent genuine advances. Fourth, the paper focuses on accuracy and speed but does not analyze numerical stability of the projection operations—repeated projections can accumulate floating-point errors especially for ill-conditioned matrices. Fifth, the experimental matrices used appear to be synthetic or from limited domains; real-world streaming matrix applications (e.g., collaborative filtering, streaming PCA) are not evaluated.
all_issues_raised: Computational complexity analysis as function of matrix size and rank is absent
Error accumulation over multiple sequential updates not analyzed
Baseline comparison may exclude competitive existing incremental SVD methods
Numerical stability of repeated projection operations not analyzed
Evaluation on synthetic matrices only; real-world streaming applications not tested

round_transcripts.critic: The projection-based truncated SVD update algorithm has several methodological issues. First, the paper claims improved computational efficiency but does not provide a rigorous theoretical analysis of the computational complexity as a function of matrix size, rank, and update frequency—without this analysis, the claimed speedups may not generalize beyond the experimental configurations tested.
